root_motivation: PRD-agentic-memory §A items 4/5/7/8/9 (touch/update/gc/stats/lineage) — operational maintenance commands missing from recall v0.1 that /self-review hand-rolls today. Like recall-doctor (slice 1 of v0.2), this ships standalone alongside the deployed v0.1 binary; full absorption into a v0.2 recall binary is later work. Each subcommand reads and (where applicable) writes the index + .md store using sqlite3 shell-out, preserving the existing schema byte-compatibly.
user_persona: jsy and /self-review consuming `recall-ops touch <id>...` to bump recall_count, `recall-ops gc --older-than 30d --dry-run|--apply` for retention, `recall-ops stats` for overview, `recall-ops lineage <id>` to walk supersedes chains, `recall-ops update <id> --body` for edits. Output: text default, JSON on --format json.

acceptance_criteria.description: `recall-ops touch <id>` bumps `recall_count` by 1 and sets `last_recalled_at` to now (RFC3339) in `memories_meta` for the given id. Exits 0 on success, 1 if the id is unknown.
`recall-ops touch <id1> <id2>...` accepts multiple ids; `--format json` returns `{touched: N, results: [{id, new_count, last_recalled_at}, ...]}`. Default text format prints one line per id.

`recall-ops gc --older-than <duration> [--never-recalled]` lists candidate memory ids matching the predicate (created_at + last_recalled_at older than now-duration; if --never-recalled, also requires recall_count=0). Without --apply, prints candidates and exits 0 (dry-run is default).

`recall-ops gc ... --apply` removes the .md file at memories_meta.path AND deletes the row from memories_meta for each candidate. Reports `{deleted: N, ids: [...]}` in JSON mode. Refuses to run without --older-than to prevent accidental wipe.

`recall-ops stats [--format text|json]` reports `{total, by_subject: {<subject>: N}, by_kind: {<kind>: N}, oldest_created: <RFC3339 or null>, distinct_embedder_ids: [...]}` from memories_meta. Deterministic JSON output.

`recall-ops lineage <id> [--format text|json]` walks `supersedes_json` (a JSON array of ids stored on each row) bidirectionally: ancestors (this row supersedes …) and descendants (this row is superseded by …). Returns chain entries with id, kind, subject, created_at. 1-deep chain returns 1 entry; cycles are broken at first repeat.
`recall-ops update <id> --confidence <0..1>` updates the row's confidence value in-place. `--body <text>` rewrites the .md body (preserving frontmatter). Exit 1 on unknown id, 0 on success.
All subcommands accept `--root <dir>` (default `~/.claude/recall`). Missing/unreadable root → exit 2 with stderr diagnostic. Missing sqlite3 binary → exit 2.
Exit codes: 0 success, 1 expected failure (unknown id, no candidates, dry-run with no matches), 2 invocation error. Stable across runs on identical inputs.
`stats` and `lineage` outputs are byte-identical across consecutive runs on the same fixture. Sorts (by_subject, by_kind keys, lineage entries) are deterministic.

scope: Rust 2024 CLI crate `recall-ops` producing a `recall-ops` binary.
Subcommands: touch, gc, stats, lineage, update.
Shell-out to sqlite3 for index reads + writes.
Direct file I/O for .md body rewrites (update --body) and deletions (gc --apply).
Two output formats: human-readable text + structured JSON.
Acceptance + proptest fixtures committed under tests/fixtures/.
non_goals: Not the full v0.2 rebuild. Companion to deployed v0.1 binary; absorbed later.
No new index columns; uses existing memories_meta schema.
No FastEmbed integration (that's a separate slice).
No `recall query` filter additions (those need recall binary edits).
No SQLite library linking (rusqlite); sqlite3 shell-out only.
No daemon, no socket, no network.
No undo on `gc --apply` (sharp tool; document and trust the external classifier).

five_whys_trace.q: Why a separate `recall-ops` binary rather than extending the deployed `recall`?
five_whys_trace.a: Same reason as recall-doctor: deployed v0.1 is stable; extending in-place requires a v0.2 rebuild that's bigger than this slice. Companion binary lands the value immediately.

five_whys_trace.q: Why combine 5 subcommands (touch, gc, stats, lineage, update) into one binary?
five_whys_trace.a: They share the same data layout, share the sqlite3 shell-out helpers, and are conceptually 'operations on existing memories.' Splitting into 5 binaries would 5x the maintenance + clutter ~/.local/bin/.
Delete-by-predicate is high-stakes. The PRD's external classifier blocks `recall delete` from automated callers; this slice preserves that posture — explicit --apply required.

five_whys_trace.q: Why update --body modify the .md file but update --confidence only the index?
five_whys_trace.a: Confidence lives only in the index row. Body lives in the .md file (with frontmatter preserved). Each subcommand touches exactly the layers it needs.

ambiguities_resolved.question: What does `touch` do if the file mtime should also bump?
ambiguities_resolved.resolution: Only the index columns (recall_count, last_recalled_at) are updated. File mtime is preserved to keep the on-disk Markdown authoritative for content (not access).

ambiguities_resolved.question: How does `lineage` resolve cycles in supersedes_json?
ambiguities_resolved.resolution: Visited set; break at first repeat. Cycles shouldn't happen in well-formed data; broken to keep the walker total.

ambiguities_resolved.question: What's the format of `supersedes_json` in memories_meta?
ambiguities_resolved.resolution: JSON array of id strings, e.g. `["01A","01B"]`. Empty array means no ancestors. Parsed best-effort; malformed values treated as empty.
